slides.title: Welcome: Writing Prompts That Actually Work
bullets: Today you will learn to write prompts that save you time on real marketing tasks
No coding, no technical background needed — just clear thinking and good writing
By the end of this session you will walk away with prompts you can use tomorrow

speaker_notes.aim: Set a safe, energetic tone and immediately make the topic feel relevant and achievable for a non-technical marketing audience.
speaker_notes.instructions: Open with a warm welcome. Emphasize that this is a hands-on session, not a lecture. Ask participants to close any AI tools they have open — they will use them intentionally later.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Think of one marketing task you did last week that felt repetitive or took longer than it should. Hold that task in mind — we are going to solve it today.
speaker_notes.debrief: Collect 2-3 examples from the room verbally. Write them on a whiteboard or sticky note to revisit during the exercise block.

slides.title: What We Will Cover Today
bullets: 09:00 – Kick-off: what prompt engineering is and why it matters for marketing
09:20 – Theory: the building blocks of a strong prompt
10:15 – Example: before/after prompts for real marketing scenarios
10:45 – Break (15 min)
11:00 – Exercise: write and test your own prompts in pairs
12:00 – Wrap-up: your personal prompt toolkit and next steps

speaker_notes.aim: Give participants a clear map of the day so they can manage their energy and know when the active parts are coming.
speaker_notes.instructions: Walk through the agenda line by line. Point out that more than half the session is hands-on. Mention that there is a break at 10:45 so people can plan accordingly.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Is there anything on the agenda you are especially curious or nervous about?
speaker_notes.debrief: Acknowledge any concerns briefly. If someone says they are nervous about the exercise, reassure them they will work in pairs and there are no wrong answers.

slides.title: Energizer: The Vague Request Game
bullets: Round 1 — Someone gives a vague instruction: 'Make something nice for social media'
Round 2 — The same person gives a specific instruction with context, goal, tone, and format
Observe: how does the quality of your mental image change between rounds?
This is exactly what happens when you give a vague vs. specific prompt to an AI tool

speaker_notes.aim: Create an experiential moment that makes the core lesson — specificity drives quality — memorable before the theory begins.
speaker_notes.instructions: Split participants into pairs. Person A gives a vague instruction, Person B tries to act on it mentally or sketch it. Then Person A gives a detailed version. Debrief as a group. Keep it light and fun.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What information did you wish you had in round one that you got in round two?
speaker_notes.debrief: Bridge to the next block: 'What you just experienced is exactly the problem prompt engineering solves. Let us look at how to do it systematically.'

slides.title: What Is Prompt Engineering?
bullets: A prompt is the instruction you give to an AI tool like ChatGPT, Gemini, or Copilot
Prompt engineering means deliberately designing that instruction to get the output you need
The AI does not read your mind — it responds to exactly what you write
Better prompt = less editing, fewer reruns, more usable output on the first try
It is a skill, not a talent — it improves with a simple framework and practice

speaker_notes.aim: Define the concept precisely and immediately connect it to the pain the audience already knows: getting bad or generic output from AI tools.
speaker_notes.instructions: Ask for a show of hands: who has used ChatGPT or a similar tool and been disappointed by the result? Use that energy. Explain that disappointment is almost always caused by the prompt, not the tool.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: When you got a bad result from an AI tool, what did you do next? Did you change the tool or change what you typed?
speaker_notes.debrief: Most people change the tool. Redirect: changing the prompt is almost always faster and more effective. That is what today is about.

slides.title: The 5 Elements of a Strong Prompt
bullets: 1. ROLE — Tell the AI who it is: 'You are a senior copywriter specializing in B2B SaaS'
2. TASK — State exactly what you want it to do: 'Write three subject lines for a re-engagement email'
3. CONTEXT — Give background: audience, product, campaign goal, brand voice
4. FORMAT — Specify the output shape: bullet list, 280-character tweet, 3 options, table
5. CONSTRAINTS — Define what to avoid: no jargon, no exclamation marks, under 50 words

speaker_notes.aim: Give participants a concrete, memorable framework they can apply immediately without needing to understand how AI works technically.
speaker_notes.instructions: Write the acronym RTCFC on the board or use the slide. Go through each element with a one-sentence example. Pause after each one and ask if it is clear. Do not rush — this is the foundation for everything else.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Looking at these five elements, which one do you think you currently leave out most often when you use AI tools?
speaker_notes.debrief: Common answer: context and constraints. Validate this and explain that most generic AI output is caused by missing context — the AI makes assumptions when you leave gaps.

slides.title: Why Context Is Your Most Powerful Element
bullets: AI tools have no memory of your brand, your audience, or your campaign unless you tell them
Context answers: Who is reading this? What do they already know? What action should they take?
Include: target audience profile, product or service name, campaign objective, brand tone of voice
Example context block: 'Our audience is HR managers at companies with 50-200 employees. They are time-poor and skeptical of vendor claims. Our tone is direct, human, and never salesy.'
You can save a standard context block and paste it into every prompt — this alone saves 20+ minutes per week

speaker_notes.aim: Elevate context from a nice-to-have to the most critical element, and introduce the practical concept of a reusable context block.
speaker_notes.instructions: Show the example context block and ask: if you gave an AI only this information, what could it already do better? Let 2-3 people respond. Then introduce the idea of saving a personal context block as a text snippet or document.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: If you had to write a three-sentence context block for your team's main product or service right now, what would you include?
speaker_notes.debrief: Give participants 2 minutes to draft a quick context block in their notes. Tell them they will use it in the exercise. This creates continuity and investment.

slides.title: Iterating: Your First Prompt Is a Draft
bullets: Do not expect a perfect result on the first try — treat the first output as a starting point
After the first output, identify what is missing, wrong, or too generic
Use follow-up prompts to refine: 'Make it shorter', 'Use a more urgent tone', 'Give me 3 alternatives'
Keep the conversation going in one chat window — the AI builds on previous context
A good prompt session is a dialogue, not a single command

speaker_notes.aim: Shift the mental model from 'one perfect prompt' to an iterative conversation, which reduces frustration and improves results dramatically.
speaker_notes.instructions: Compare it to briefing a junior colleague: you rarely get exactly what you want on the first try, and that is normal. You give feedback and they improve. AI works the same way. Emphasize that follow-up prompts are short and fast.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: When you have gotten a bad first result from an AI, how many times did you actually follow up before giving up?
speaker_notes.debrief: Most people give up after one try. Normalize iteration. Tell them: 'In the exercise you will practice this — give a first prompt, evaluate the output, then refine it at least twice.'

slides.title: Common Prompt Mistakes Marketers Make
bullets: Too vague: 'Write something about our product launch' — no role, no audience, no format
Too open-ended: asking for 'a great email' without defining what great means for your brand
No constraints: forgetting to specify word count, platform, or what NOT to include
Asking for everything at once: one huge prompt for a full campaign — break it into steps
Accepting the first draft: copying output without reading it critically or fact-checking claims

speaker_notes.aim: Normalize mistakes and give participants a checklist of things to actively avoid, making the framework stickier through contrast.
speaker_notes.instructions: Present each mistake as something that happens to everyone, including experienced users. For each one, ask: 'Has anyone here done this? Raise your hand — I have too.' Use humor to keep energy up.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Which of these mistakes do you think will be hardest to break? Why?
speaker_notes.debrief: The most common answer is 'accepting the first draft.' Reinforce the critical reading habit: AI output is a first draft, not a finished product. You are still the creative director.

slides.title: Prompt Engineering for Marketing: Your Use Cases
bullets: Social media: writing post variations per platform (LinkedIn vs. Instagram vs. X) in one session
Email marketing: subject line testing, body copy drafts, re-engagement sequences
SEO content: blog outlines, meta descriptions, FAQ sections based on keyword input
Campaign briefs: turning a bullet list of ideas into a structured creative brief
Internal comms: translating campaign strategy into team updates or stakeholder summaries

speaker_notes.aim: Make the theory immediately tangible by anchoring it to marketing tasks the audience recognizes from their own workday.
speaker_notes.instructions: Ask participants to mark which use case is most relevant to them. This primes them for the exercise. If the group has a specific focus (e.g., only social media), emphasize those examples more heavily.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Which of these use cases would save you the most time if you could do it in half the time with better output?
speaker_notes.debrief: Collect 3-4 answers. These will naturally lead into the example block. Transition: 'Let us see exactly what a good prompt looks like for some of these scenarios.'

slides.title: Before & After: Social Media Post
bullets: BEFORE — Weak prompt: 'Write a LinkedIn post about our new product feature'
OUTPUT: Generic, corporate-sounding post with no specific detail or audience hook

AFTER — Strong prompt: 'You are a B2B content strategist. Write a LinkedIn post for HR managers at mid-sized companies announcing a new AI-powered onboarding feature. Tone: conversational and confident, not salesy. Format: hook line, 3 short paragraphs, one call to action. Max 200 words. Do not use buzzwords like revolutionary or game-changing.'
OUTPUT: Specific, on-brand, ready to publish with minimal editing
Difference: 7 extra sentences in the prompt saved 20 minutes of editing

speaker_notes.aim: Show the framework in action with a realistic marketing scenario so participants can see the direct link between prompt quality and output quality.
speaker_notes.instructions: If you have live internet access, run this prompt in real-time and show the output on screen. The live demonstration is far more powerful than a pre-written example. Ask participants to evaluate the output together.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Looking at the strong prompt, which of the 5 elements from our framework can you spot?
speaker_notes.debrief: Walk through each element as participants identify them: Role (B2B content strategist), Task (write a LinkedIn post), Context (HR managers, mid-sized companies, new feature), Format (hook, 3 paragraphs, CTA, 200 words), Constraints (no buzzwords, not salesy).

slides.title: Before & After: Email Subject Lines
bullets: BEFORE — Weak prompt: 'Give me subject lines for our summer campaign email'
OUTPUT: 5 generic lines like 'Summer Sale is Here!' and 'Don't Miss Out This Summer'

AFTER — Strong prompt: 'You are an email marketing specialist. Write 5 subject lines for a re-engagement email targeting subscribers who have not opened in 90 days. Product: an online project management tool for freelancers. Goal: get them to try the new time-tracking feature. Tone: friendly and slightly playful. Each subject line must be under 45 characters and avoid spam trigger words like free or urgent.'
OUTPUT: 5 distinct, testable subject lines with clear hooks and no filler words
Bonus iteration prompt: 'Now write 3 versions of line 2 with an even stronger curiosity hook'

speaker_notes.aim: Demonstrate iteration in addition to the initial framework, showing how a follow-up prompt refines a specific element without starting over.
speaker_notes.instructions: Run this live if possible. After showing the strong output, actually send the bonus iteration prompt and show the result. This makes the iteration concept concrete and fast — participants see it takes 10 seconds to refine.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: How would you adapt this prompt for your own product or campaign? What would you change in the context section?
speaker_notes.debrief: Ask 1-2 participants to suggest their adaptation verbally. This bridges the example to the upcoming exercise and shows the framework is transferable.

slides.title: Before & After: Campaign Brief from Bullet Points
bullets: SCENARIO: You have a messy list of campaign ideas from a brainstorm session and need a structured brief
BEFORE — Weak prompt: 'Turn these notes into a campaign brief' + paste of unstructured bullets
OUTPUT: Slightly reorganized version of your notes, still unclear and incomplete

AFTER — Strong prompt: 'You are a marketing strategist. Turn the following brainstorm notes into a structured campaign brief. Include these sections: Campaign objective, Target audience, Key message, Channel plan, Tone of voice, and Success metrics. Write in clear professional English. Flag any gaps in the notes where information is missing. Notes: [paste notes here]'
OUTPUT: A professional brief with clear sections, and a list of questions that reveal what was missing from the original notes

speaker_notes.aim: Show a use case beyond writing copy — using AI as a thinking partner and structured document builder, which is often a surprise to marketing teams.
speaker_notes.instructions: Ask participants: how much time do you spend cleaning up notes and formatting documents after meetings? This use case typically gets a strong reaction. Emphasize the 'flag gaps' instruction as a powerful technique — the AI tells you what you do not know yet.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What other messy or unstructured documents in your daily work could benefit from a structuring prompt like this?
speaker_notes.debrief: Collect 2-3 examples: meeting notes, client feedback, competitor research dumps. Note them for the wrap-up where participants build their personal prompt toolkit. Then announce the break.

slides.title: The Iteration Loop in Practice
bullets: Step 1 — Send your structured prompt and read the output critically: what is good, what is off?
Step 2 — Identify ONE thing to fix first: tone, length, angle, or structure
Step 3 — Send a focused follow-up: 'The tone is too formal. Rewrite using shorter sentences and a more direct voice'
Step 4 — Repeat until the output needs only minor manual edits
Rule of thumb: if you need more than 4 iterations, your original prompt was missing key context — go back and add it

speaker_notes.aim: Give participants a concrete repeatable loop they can use in the exercise and in their daily work, turning iteration from abstract advice into a numbered process.
speaker_notes.instructions: Draw or show the loop visually: Prompt → Read → Identify → Refine → Read → Done. Ask participants to keep this loop in mind during the exercise. Mention the 4-iteration rule as a useful self-check.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What makes it hard to evaluate AI output critically? What might you miss or accept too quickly?
speaker_notes.debrief: Common answers: reading too fast, being relieved to have something, not checking facts. Validate all of these. Reinforce: you are still the expert and the editor. AI is your assistant, not your decision-maker.

slides.title: Exercise Introduction: Build Your Own Prompt
bullets: You will work in pairs for 45 minutes
Each person picks ONE real marketing task they do regularly — the one you thought of at the start of the day
Step 1: Write a weak prompt (2 min) — just your instinct, no framework
Step 2: Run it and read the output critically together
Step 3: Rewrite using the 5-element framework (Role, Task, Context, Format, Constraints)
Step 4: Run the strong prompt and iterate at least twice using follow-up prompts
Step 5: Note what changed between your weak and strong prompt outputs

speaker_notes.aim: Set up the exercise clearly so participants spend time practicing, not figuring out what to do. Every minute of confusion is a wasted practice minute.
speaker_notes.time: 5 minutes for setup, 45 minutes for the exercise
speaker_notes.instructions: Pair participants intentionally — mix people who work on different tasks so they can learn from each other's use cases. Make sure everyone has access to ChatGPT, Gemini, or whichever tool your organization uses. Distribute the prompt framework card if you have a printed reference.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Before you start, tell your partner: what is the task you chose and what output do you hope to get?
speaker_notes.debrief: This pre-exercise share makes participants commit to a goal and gives them a benchmark to evaluate their results against. Circulate the room and offer coaching to pairs who get stuck.

slides.title: Prompt Framework Reference Card
bullets: ROLE: 'You are a [specific role with relevant expertise]...'
TASK: 'Write / Create / Summarize / Rewrite / Draft [specific deliverable]...'
CONTEXT: Audience profile + product/service + campaign goal + brand tone
FORMAT: Specify structure, length, number of options, platform requirements
CONSTRAINTS: What to avoid, what NOT to say, tone limits, character counts
ITERATION starters: 'Make it shorter' / 'Use a more urgent tone' / 'Give me 3 alternatives' / 'Flag what is missing'

speaker_notes.aim: Provide a quick-reference tool participants can keep open on screen or printed next to them during the exercise and in their daily work.
speaker_notes.time: 2 minutes to present, then displayed throughout the exercise
speaker_notes.instructions: Tell participants this slide will stay on the shared screen during the exercise. If you are in a physical room, consider printing this as a card. Encourage them to annotate it with their own examples as they work.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Which of these elements feels most natural to write? Which feels hardest?
speaker_notes.debrief: Use answers to anticipate where pairs will need coaching during the exercise. Constraints and context are typically the hardest — watch for those in your rounds.

slides.title: Coaching Checkpoints During the Exercise
bullets: After Step 2 (weak prompt output): Does the output feel generic? That is expected — that is the point
After Step 3 (rewriting): Does your strong prompt include all 5 elements? Read it out loud to check
After Step 4 (strong prompt output): Compare side by side — what specifically improved?
Stuck? Ask yourself: what does the AI not know about my audience or goal that I forgot to include?
Done early? Try a second task, or add an iteration that changes the tone or platform

speaker_notes.aim: Give participants self-coaching tools so they can diagnose their own sticking points without needing the trainer for every question.
speaker_notes.instructions: Read through these checkpoints before the exercise starts. Tell participants to use them as a checklist. During the exercise, use these questions yourself when you visit pairs: 'Where are you right now? Have you compared the two outputs yet?'
speaker_notes.reflective_question: At checkpoint 4: what is ONE concrete thing your strong prompt produced that your weak prompt did not?
speaker_notes.debrief: Use this question during the full-group debrief at the end of the exercise. Ask each pair to share their one thing — this creates a rapid-fire set of insights that reinforces learning for the whole room.

slides.title: Exercise Debrief: Share Your Results
bullets: Each pair shares: the task they chose, their weak prompt, and their strong prompt
Group observes: which element made the biggest difference in that pair's output?
Collect the best iteration prompts from the room — what follow-up instructions worked well?
What surprised you? What did not work as expected?
What would you do differently if you ran this prompt again tomorrow?

speaker_notes.aim: Convert individual learning into shared knowledge by surfacing patterns across pairs — the best insights in the room often come from comparing different use cases.
speaker_notes.instructions: Ask 3-4 pairs to share. For each pair, ask the group to identify which of the 5 elements had the most impact. Write the best iteration prompts on a shared screen or whiteboard — these become a group prompt library.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: If you could share one thing you learned from your partner's prompt that you will use in your own work, what would it be?
speaker_notes.debrief: Synthesize the debrief: 'What I am hearing is that context and constraints consistently made the biggest difference. That tracks with what we said in the theory block — the AI needs to know your audience and your limits.' Transition to wrap-up.

slides.title: What You Can Do Now That You Could Not Do This Morning
bullets: Write a structured prompt using Role, Task, Context, Format, and Constraints for any marketing task
Diagnose a bad AI output and identify which element was missing from your prompt
Run an iteration loop to improve output without starting over from scratch
Build a reusable context block for your brand that shortens every future prompt you write
Use AI as a thinking partner — not just a text generator — for briefs, outlines, and structuring messy input

speaker_notes.aim: Anchor learning by contrasting where participants started versus where they are now — this creates a felt sense of progress and builds confidence.
speaker_notes.instructions: Read each bullet and ask participants to physically nod or raise a hand if they feel confident about that skill. If a skill gets low confidence, briefly revisit the key point in one sentence. Do not re-teach — just remind.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: On a scale of 1-5, how confident do you feel writing a prompt for your most common marketing task right now versus this morning?
speaker_notes.debrief: Most participants will score higher than they expected. Celebrate that. If someone scores low, ask what would make them more confident — usually it is more practice, which leads naturally to the next steps slide.

slides.title: Your Prompt Toolkit: Take This Into Your Week
bullets: Save your best prompt from today as a template — add it to a shared team folder
Write your brand context block this week and share it with your team: one consistent context saves everyone time
Challenge: use a structured prompt for every AI task for the next 5 working days — track what changes
When you get a bad output, do not abandon the tool — audit your prompt against the 5 elements
Maverx resource: [link to prompt template library] — pre-built prompts for the 10 most common marketing tasks

speaker_notes.aim: Convert today's learning into specific actions with a clear timeline so that knowledge does not fade before it becomes habit.
speaker_notes.instructions: Walk through each action item. Ask participants to write down the ONE action they will take before end of day today — not end of week, today. This specificity dramatically increases follow-through. If you have a shared channel (Teams, Slack), encourage them to post their context block there for peer accountability.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What is the one action from this list you will do before you open your laptop tomorrow morning?
speaker_notes.debrief: Ask each participant to say their one action out loud or type it in the chat. Public commitment increases follow-through. Note the actions you hear — you can follow up in a team channel next week.

slides.title: Closing: You Are Still the Creative Director
bullets: AI tools are fast, but they do not know your brand, your customers, or your judgment — you do
A great prompt does not replace your expertise — it lets your expertise work faster
The best marketers using AI today are not the most technical — they are the clearest communicators
Keep experimenting: every prompt you write makes the next one better
Thank you — questions, feedback, and your best prompts are welcome in [team channel]

speaker_notes.aim: End with an empowering message that positions participants as the skilled professionals in the human-AI partnership, not subordinates to the tool.
speaker_notes.instructions: Deliver this slide with energy and conviction. Make eye contact. This is the emotional close of the session — it should leave people feeling capable and motivated, not overwhelmed. Open the floor for final questions.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What is one way you think prompt engineering could change how your team works together — not just individually?
speaker_notes.debrief: If time allows, let 2-3 people answer this team-level question. It surfaces collaboration opportunities like a shared prompt library or a team context block. Close by reminding participants of the Maverx follow-up resource and where to ask questions after today.
